title: Bill Maris: How Google Could Crush AI Competitors, Why Small Funds Win, and AI's Atari Stage

segments.text: After saying he was out, now Bill Maris is returning to the investing world.
The founding CEO of Google Ventress has raised $150 million for his new fund called Section
With a smaller fund, I have the advantage to be very selective in the companies that I
invested in, the people that I hire.
We're going to invest for a financial return.
Any other metric is impossible to measure and therefore won't succeed.
Think of the change that has happened just in the last 100 years and what's about
to happen in the next 100 years with the advent of AI, the world's in the change by
orders of magnitude.
Thank you very much for that warm welcome.
I am Bill Maris.
I'm the founder of Section 32.
Prior to that, I was the founder and CEO of Google Ventress.
I was also Google's Vice President of Special Projects where I incubated Waymo and Google
Projects, Calico and many other projects as well and before that I founded a web hosting
and data center company which we're going to talk a little bit about and today I think
I'm going to talk to you about a few of the lessons I've learned on these interesting
experiences I've had in life so we'll start with four lessons I'm going to talk about
and we're going to go back to 1997 to start when I was a fresh college graduate I had
a degree in neuroscience and I found myself on Wall Street somehow managed to land a
job there but I was miserable having to wear a suit and try to work in the heat but one
good thing came of that which was I looked in the closet of the office one day and I saw
a server and I asked well what is this thing beneath our jackets and they said well that's
where our email and websites live and as can happen to many of us I had a moment where
I felt like I was bathed in the light of inspiration and I thought I think I've glimpsed
the future I think I can maybe make a business out of this because if you can have our website
and email in your closet how many websites and emails could I put in my closet so I immediately
quit my job because I had kind of glimpsed through a keyhole and through that keyhole I thought I
saw the internet and I saw a data center and it looked something like this or maybe when I say
data center you think of something like this or something like this but in 1997 a state of the
art data center looked almost exactly like this we had three servers a small medium and large
business grew we eventually had five servers and this isn't a data center all this was my
apartment where I founded the company with credit cards and the servers lived in one room the work
happened in the other room and we get very hot in that room and this was in Vermont so I open
the windows and then we get very cold so cold in fact that by noon if you had a glass of water
in your desk it would ice over you may think though this isn't so bad but actually this was also
my apartment as well this was the bed and you may look at that and think well you've got a mattress
and a nice pillow and then look at that nice blanket but this is a rug I got from Home Depot to keep
myself warm on those nights and one day there was a thunderstorm the roof started to leak and I
knew I needed to do something because water and computers and servers don't mix well so I called
the landlord and said the roof leaking the landlord said well that happens sometimes but I knew
that I needed to do something so when you don't know what to do you go to Home Depot I got a bucket
of tar and a mop and I went up on the roof and there was lightning and there was rain and I went
up there and I I tarred the roof and I did not glimpse the future in that case because I didn't know
when you're tearing the roof that you should start at the far corner and work towards the door rather
than the reverse and I tarred myself into a corner but the choice that I faced was either the servers get
electrocuted or perhaps I get electrocuted but as an entrepreneur I was willing to take that risk
which you know newsflash I survived my shoes though are still stuck on that roof in Vermont which
takes me to lesson two which is to see the future sometimes you need to be a little bit insane
it may appear to those around you that you are tearing the roof in the thunderstorm and to that
point I'm going to share a few slides here that a friend named Stuart Butterfield was kind enough
to share with me and here's the inauguration 1989 and there's someone taking a picture that makes
sense it's probably a film camera and 2005 is not very different there's still someone back there
taking a picture and then let's go just four years later to another inauguration and if we look
closely it's quite a bit different because now everybody's got a camera everybody's got a camera
and this was kind of before cameras were mushed into cell phones it was kind of around that time
it was starting to happen but but that's not the most interesting thing about this photo because in
this crowd is someone who to his friends I'm sure seemed insane who also did glimpse the future
and we look closely this gentleman has decided to I don't know live stream or record the inauguration on
his laptop he knew something that those around him didn't know which is one of the things that I've
always looked for entrepreneurs is they know a secret about the future that most of us don't believe
let's fast forward to 2007 and find myself somehow at Google and a challenge was given to me
the challenge was Google needs a vendor fund we were starting to make some investments we didn't
have a coherent strategy there were no budgets I had to figure out what to do so I first found a
friend rich minor is the co-founder of Android and he became my partner in crime as we conceptualized
what what could Google ventures be we went up and down Sand Hill Road and we we talked to
everyone anyone that was willing to talk to us and have a conversation we were willing to
talk to to see what we what we could learn we came up with a plan our plan was to obtain all the
data of venture that we could find and being Google you can imagine it was a lot of data historical
data you name it then we decided we would step to use AI but at that time Google would not let us
use the term AI and this persisted for many years bill AI is science fiction it is it's a hundred
years away if it's ever going to happen let's stick to machine learning by the way when you say AI
it freaks people out so stop freaking people out so we had to call machine learning and we used
machine learning to do two things design the ideal portfolio construction by running millions and
millions of simulations and back testing and all of the things you can imagine that data scientists
would do and and to determine what the ideal fund size would be and people were excited here's
a headline from TechCrunch at the time and and people inside of Google were also pretty excited
this is one of the senior execs I later learned had this to say and you know I I have to admit
it seemed crazy the plan seemed crazy at the time but let's look at how it turned out so over
this time period a two thousand ninety two two thousand eighteen top quartile VC returns look like
this and top death style looked like this using publicly available information I'm not sharing any
non public for prior to a Google information we would estimate Google Ventures returns at about
4.1x and I adhered more closely to the strategy and the investments that I led and the investments
that I led turned out like this which takes me to lesson three which is don't bet against computer
science I've seen it happen many many times in many many fields if you apply the right kind of
computer science at the right time to the right problem you will get to the right answers I would not
bet against it even if it looks like you're tiring the roof in a thunderstorm so let's fast forward
to two thousand and seventeen I decide to start my own fund and again those around me said
you're insane why would you do that you're in the warm womb of Google lunches free and the
massages are plenty and so forth but after the idea you know sunk in I the advice turned into
raises much money as possible you know that's the right way to run a fund you'll get a big
management fee you'll be happy things are going to work out really well for you and I thought about
that relative to everything I had done up to that point and I decided to to not take that advice
and then over the course of my time at section 32 we've had six funds we've invested in companies
like CrowdStrike and Co here and Coinbase and all six of those funds have averaged about 400
million in size and all are performing their top desial and to the extent there is DPI to measure
that's the only measure as far as I'm concerned and venture that counts as DPI which takes me to
lesson four that this will be heresy to some but small funds outperform large funds this is simply
the math this is not an opinion I'm trying to convince you of but there are many reasons for this
smaller funds you can have more focus you have I mean I've I've already managed a multi-billion
dollar fund with hundreds of employees it's distracting you cannot give the attention to founders that
I would like to give it and there are many reasons for this and if we look at top desial performance
of DPI fund smaller than 750 million average return of 4.76x and funds larger than a billion
2.42x funds below 750 million across that time period represented 95% of top desial performers with
discontinuous return compression above 750 million why is this there's a lot of reasons for this
you can use your own numbers but I'll just do a little thought experiment if you have a 500
million dollar fund and let's say on average these days you can own 10% of a company
you need five billion dollars of exits to get your money back let's just remind ourselves that the
75th percentile venture loses money and there is persistence of performance of the top quartile

of exit value in your companies now if you have a seven billion dollar fund and we do the same math
you know you've got to return 210 billion 7 billion to 70 times 3x is 210 billion which
exceeds the total venture-backed M&A and IPO exit value in most years
this year may be an exception but that is something I'm looking forward to talking about when we

you guys are old friends yes we are we go way back we'll bills when he started Google Ventures

I some might say a flight of capital to India and China right now are you seeing that that the

the US has always indexed on human safety over speed to market and that has cost us in some

so there's a balance there but there's certainly there's research going on in China and other

We really need the neurological reserves here and this business with or brain trust with the

What areas like that are you focused on investing in? I mean human biology and healthcare it's

That's good. I want to bring I want to bring Saxon freeberg before we run out of time if

to be going back into the venture business but I'm curious your take on when you started

the five hundred million dollar fund sweet spot or do you think you need to become
Andreessen Horowitz when you go back to the private sector? Well I don't think we need to become

portfolio diversification and construction that'll determine your check size and that's where
it determines where you play in the market. The thing that's spinning through my head after
Thomas's presentation is you know are you better off just focusing on you know let's call it
what used to be called I don't know late venture early growth you know you're writing 50 million
dollar checks you just kind of wait for the breakouts as opposed to playing in this really noisy
super early stage game. I think the problem with that yeah is we have to look at the incentive
structure of venture so a five billion dollar venture fund that returns 1.01 x gets to say that
they are in the 75th percentile and can raise their next fund and no one at the Stanford
Endowment is going to get in trouble for writing that check they need to put two or five hundred
million into a fund multiple times so so I understand that dynamic so now let's look at the
GP dynamic well if I have a five billion dollar fund I return 1.01 x I'm going to make more money
than bill with his five hundred million dollar fund that returns three x okay so that's also a
strange incentive so now let's look at the entrepreneur side I am researcher x from open AI I'm

you're a seasoned entrepreneur who has kind of been down the road and knows the pitfalls of that
and so the incentives are broken in all those ways and the pendulum will swing back so I don't
think just staying late stage and waiting to sniper at larger companies will be a long term the
data would suggest that's not going to work in the long term okay let's thank bill amazing job
